revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八色の姓]][[ko:팔색성]][[zh:八色姓]]


{{merge|kabane|qq=}}
{{Refimprove|date=2016th year 11月6日 (日) 13:20 (UTC)|qq=}}
'''Yakusa no Kabane''' (八色の姓) refers to the eight Ranks of [[kabane]] (clan titles) established by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} in the 13th year of his reign (684 CE). This system is also known as the '''Eight Kabane''' (八姓, ''Hasshō'') or the '''Tenmu Eight Kabane''' (天武八姓, ''Tenmu Hasshō'').
The eight kabane, in descending order of Rank, are: {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|qid=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), [[Sukune]] (宿禰), [[Imiki]] (忌寸), [[Michinoshi]] (道師), {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|qid=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}} (臣), [[Muraji]] (連), and {{ill|Inagi|en|Inagi|qid=Q317847|lt=Inagi|1=Inagi}} (稲置).
== Overview ==
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' entry for the 13th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}'s reign, on the first day of the 10th month, states: 
> "An edict was issued to revise the clans’ kabane, creating the eight Ranks of kabane to unify the myriad clan titles in the realm. The first is Mahito, the second is Asomi, the third is Sukune, the fourth is Imiki, the fifth is Michinoshi, the sixth is Omi, the seventh is Muraji, and the eighth is Inagi."{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=27|qq=}}.
On the same day, 13 clans, including Mori no Kimi, Michi no Kimi, Takahashi no Kimi, Mikuni no Kimi, Taima no Kimi, Ibaraki no Kimi, {{ill|Tajihi clan|en|Tajihi clan|qid=Q11430839|lt=Tajihi no Kimi|1=Tajihi clan}}, Ina no Kimi, Sakata no Kimi, {{ill|Okinaga Clan|lt=Okinaga Clan|lt=Oshikaga no Kimi|ja|息長氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikaga no Kimi|qq=}}, Haneda no Kimi, Sakebito no Kimi, and Yamamichi no Kimi, were granted the kabane of Mahito.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=27|qq=}} On the first day of the 11th month, 52 clans, including {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi|ja|大三輪君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were granted the kabane of Asomi. On the first day of the 12th month, 50 clans, including {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|qid=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo no Muraji|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, were granted the kabane of Sukune. In the 14th year of Tenmu's reign (685), 11 clans, including {{ill|Yamato no Muraji|ja|大和連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Muraji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were granted the kabane of Imiki.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=27|qq=}} However, no records exist of clans being granted the kabane of Michinoshi or Inagi.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=35|qq=}}
The kabane of Mahito was granted exclusively to clans holding the kabane of Kimi, descendants of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} or, more frequently, descendants of emperors within five generations of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=36-38|qq=}} Of the 52 clans granted the kabane of Asomi, 40 previously held the kabane of Omi, 10 held Kimi, and 2 held Muraji. These clans were more distantly related to the imperial lineage.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=37-38|qq=}} The kabane of Sukune was granted primarily to clans holding the kabane of Muraji, many of whom claimed descent from the celestial deities (''tenshin'') or their descendants.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=37|qq=}} 
The kabane of Imiki has been theorized to have been granted to descendants of {{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}} (immigrants), but only about half (5 clans) fit this description. Most recipients had previously held the kabane of ''na'' (直), ''kō'' (造), or ''kishi'' (吉士), and had been elevated to Muraji prior to receiving the kabane of Imiki.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=38|qq=}}
The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' records that in the third year of the [[Tenpyō-Hōji]] era (759 CE), all instances of the kabane of Kimi were changed to Kō, and ''Imiki'' was standardized as ''Imiki''. This change was likely intended to restrict the use of "Kimi," a term closely associated with the emperor, and to eliminate the "Mi" character from {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|qid=Q540305|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro}}’s title, ''藤原恵美押勝''.{{sfn|Nito Atsushi|2021|p=148|qq=}}
== New Social Order ==
The ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' interprets the Eight Kabane as a reward for loyalty during the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|qid=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}. However, scholars such as [[Ōta Ryō]], [[Kuroita Katsumi]], and [[Sakamoto Tarō]] view the Eight Kabane as part of Emperor Tenmu’s effort to restructure the clan system.{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=29-32|qq=}} 
According to Kuroita Katsumi, this reform was akin to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} division of society into [[kazoku]] (peers), {{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|qid=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku|1=Shizoku}} (gentry), and [[heimin]] (commoners).{{sfn|Takeuchi Risan|1950|p=30|qq=}} By introducing the kabane of Mahito, Asomi, and Sukune, Emperor Tenmu extracted select clans with strong ties to the imperial family from the Ranks of Omi and Muraji, creating a new social hierarchy that distinguished central nobles from regional elites.
The Eight Kabane thus marked a transition from a {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} to a centralized bureaucratic state. 
== Transition to Ritsuryō Bureaucracy ==
The introduction of the Eight Kabane in 684 occurred alongside the drafting of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qid=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} in 680. Both reforms reflect Emperor Tenmu’s effort to align the clan system with the needs of the emerging [[ritsuryō system]]. By the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the kabane of Asomi became dominant, with clans such as the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], [[Taira clan|Taira]], [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|qid=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana|1=Tachibana clan}} rising to prominence. 
== References ==
{{reflist|qq=}}
== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite journal|和書|title = The Significance of Tenmu's "Eight Kabane"|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1390290699826643456|publisher = Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|journal = ''Shien''|volume = 43|doi = 10.15017/2339018|naid = 120006725629 |issn = 03869326|author = Takeuchi Risan|authorlink = 竹内理三|year = 1950|ref=harv|qq=}}
